title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What is Life Insurance Premium?
Life can be very uncertain. And it is always a good thing to be prepared for what the future may hold. One of the ways to plan for your future is to purchase a life insurance plan. It ensure that your loved ones remains financially secure even in case something unexpected happens to you. A financial safety net can make sure that your family’s life goals are not affected negatively.
What is Life Insurance?
Every person who is new to the world of insurance has had this question - what is life insurance? In simple terms, life insurance is a contract between an individual and a life insurance company. Under this contract, the insurance company is legally bound to pay out an amount to the person nominated by the policyholder in case of the policyholder’s death.
There are different types of life insurance available in the Indian insurance market. Some, like the term insurance plan, offer a pure life cover. That means that these types of life insurance plans pay the nominee of the policyholder only on the policyholder’s death during the policy term.
Other types of life insurance, like savings plans and retirement plans come with added benefits that can help you fulfil the life goals at different life stages. These policies may also pay out the benefits if the insured person outlives the policy term. In return for these death or maturity benefits, the policyholder will have to pay life insurance premiums to the insurance company. What is the meaning of this premium? Let’s find out.
What is the Meaning of Life Insurance Premium?
Life insurance premium is the amount that a policyholder pays the insurer to enjoy life insurance coverage and other benefits. Only if the policyholder pays all the premiums on time will the insurance company honour the contract and pay the policy benefits when they are due. Depending on the terms and conditions of the policy, premiums can be paid as a lump sum amount or in equal instalments. Life insurance plans also give policyholders the option to pay the premiums on a monthly, quarterly, semi-annual, or annual basis. Again, this varies from one plan to another.
How Does an Insurance Premium Work?
When you buy an insurance policy, you pay a fixed amount of money. This amount is called a premium. As long as you pay the premium on time, your cover stays in place, which helps keep the policy active. You can choose how to pay the premium. Some people pay once a year, while some pay every month or every three months. It depends on what suits their budget. If the premium is not paid on time, the policy may lapse, resulting in no coverage.
How is Life Insurance Premium Calculated?
The life insurance premium for different types of life insurance plans depends on a number of factors. Insurance companies calculate the premium after taking into account various aspects like the gender of the applicant, their age, the policy term, the lifestyle of the applicant and sometimes, even their occupation etc.
Let’s take a closer look at how these factors affect the calculation of life insurance premium –
1. Gender
Women have been reported to have a higher life expectancy than men. So, life insurance premiums charged for women tends to be lower than the charges levied for male policyholders of the same age.
2. Age
Age, by itself, also influences the calculation of life insurance premiums. All life insurance application forms will ask you for this information. This is because your age is a direct indicator of your health and life expectancy, in most cases. As expected, younger people tend to be healthier and live longer. So, the premiums charged for them are significantly lower as compared to what’s charged for older people. The younger you are, the lower your life insurance premiums will be.
For example, say you are 25 and you just got your first job. You purchase a life insurance plan with your first salary, and the cover it offers is for 40 years. This means you will continue to pay premiums until you are 65 years of age if you do not opt for a single payment option or a limited payment option. Since 40 years is several years away, the premium charged will be significantly lower than what somebody older who wishes to purchase the same life insurance plan.
3. Policy term
The policy term is the duration over which the life insurance coverage remains in effect. Generally, the longer the policy term, the more instalments your cost is spread over. So, the premium charged per instalment will be higher for longer policy term.
4. Lifestyle
Lifestyle choices like smoking and drinking will lead to higher premiums because these habits can reduce the life expectancy, resulting in early death. Similarly, other lifestyle choices like engaging in adventurous sports that pose a high risk to life also increases the premiums.
5. Occupation
Some occupations are more dangerous than others. For example, a flight attendant’s job is riskier than the job of somebody who works at a desk in an office. Occupations involving physical risk typically lead to higher premiums.Habits
If you have smoking or drinking habits, there may be a chance that your premium amount is high. These habits are detrimental to health, so insurance companies may charge high premiums.
Medical History
If you or your close family members have had serious health problems like cancer or heart disease, your premium may be high.
Nature of Employment
Some jobs are more risky than others. For example, construction workers or those working with machines may have to pay more than office workers. Risky jobs mean higher premiums.
Likelihood of Claims Being Made by the Person Insured
If you have made insurance claims in the past or your profile shows high chances of future claims, the premium may be higher. Insurers review records before determining the premium.
Height and Weight
Your height and weight are used to find out your Body Mass Index (BMI). If your BMI is too high or too low, it may pose health risks, which might lead to a higher premium.
Marital Status and Dependents
If you're married and have children, you may want to consider higher coverage due to dependents.
Hobbies with High Risks
If you enjoy adventure sports like skydiving, deep-sea diving, or racing, your premium may be calculated as higher because they carry a higher-than-average risk of injury.
Debts
If you have many loans, you might go for higher coverage to protect your family. Higher coverage means a higher premium. Insurers may also suggest a shorter payment term to reduce risk.
Types of Insurance Premiums
There are different ways to pay your insurance premium, based on what suits your needs and budget. Let’s look at the common types of insurance premiums you may come across while purchasing one for yourself.
Life Insurance Premiums
This is the money you pay to keep your life cover active. It depends on your age, health, job, occupation, lifestyle and sum assured. If you smoke or drink, your premium may be higher. You can pay this premium monthly, yearly, or all at once, depending on your plan.
Health Insurance Premiums
This is the amount you pay to get health cover for you or your family. It protects you from hospital bills. When purchasing a premium health insurance plan, factors such as age, medical history, and plan type may affect your premium. Some employers pay this premium as a benefit for their workers.
Auto Insurance Premiums
Auto insurance premiums are what you pay to protect your car. If your car gets damaged or stolen, your insurer pays for repairs or loss. The premium might depend on factors like:
The car type (expensive cars have higher premiums)
Where you live (areas with more accidents or theft have higher rates)
Your age (young or very old drivers may pay more)
Homeowners Insurance Premiums
If you own a home, this insurance protects it from damage due to fire, storms, or theft. The premium might depend on:
The value of your home
Its size and age
The location (homes in risky areas like flood or storm zones have higher premiums)
Factors to Consider While Buying an Insurance Premium Policy?
Picking an insurance plan may depend on several things. You can think about your age, family needs, and how much you can afford to pay easily. Here are a few simple points to look at before you choose a plan:
Current Life Stage
The current stage of your life may affect your insurance needs. A single person, a married couple, or a parent with kids, each stage might have different coverage. Pick a policy that suits your current stage in life and has the potential to take care of your plans.
Financial Requirements
Think about how much your family will need in case you’re not around. Include home loans, education costs, or day-to-day expenses. Choose a cover that suits your income bracket and offers good protection without becoming a burden.
Duration of the Policy
This is the number of years your life insurance will protect you. Choose the term based on your goals. For example, if you have young children, you may want coverage until they grow up and become independent.
Riders
Riders, also known as add-ons, are extra covers you can add to your basic policy. These provide additional protection, such as coverage for critical illness, accidents, or disability. You may consider purchasing these at an extra cost to increase your coverage.
Claim Settlement Ratio
This shows how many claims an insurer has paid out compared to claims received. A high Claim Settlement Ratio means the company is good at paying what it promised. So, it is beneficial to choose a company with a promising record when it comes to the Claim Settlement Ratio.
Terms and Conditions
Always read the policy document carefully. This will help you understand what is covered and what is not. Check the document for what will happen if you miss payments, want to claim money, or cancel the plan. Understanding the terms helps you avoid surprises later.
How to Pay Life Insurance Premiums?
You can pay your life insurance premium in different ways.:
Pay online using UPI, debit card, credit card, or net banking
Set up automatic payments
Pay at the nearest insurance branch in cash or cheque
Some insurers allow payment through mobile apps
If you get insurance through work, the amount may be deducted from your salary
Choose an option that fits with your routine and helps you stay on track with your payments.
How Often Do You Pay Insurance Premiums?
You can choose how often you want to pay your premium:
Monthly
Quarterly (every 3 months)
Half-yearly (every 6 months)
Yearly (once a year)
Paying yearly might help you purchase insurance at an affordable price. But if monthly works better for your budget, that’s also fine. Choose what fits you best.
How Does Deductible Affect Insurance Premium?
A deductible is the amount you pay before the insurance company helps with a claim. Here's how it affects your premium:
If your deductible is high, your premium will be lower
If your deductible is low, your premium will be higher
Why? Because with a high deductible, you are taking on more risk, so the insurance company charges you less. But if you want the insurance company to start paying sooner, you’ll need to pay more for that benefit.
Before choosing a deductible, think about how much you can pay out of your own pocket during an emergency.
Tips to Reduce Your Life Insurance Premium
Now that you know the factors that play a key role in calculating life insurance premiums, here are some ways in which you can reduce these costs –
Ensure that you remain healthy and fit. Exercise regularly.
Quit harmful lifestyle practices like smoking and drinking.
Buy life insurance when you are younger and buy it online if you can.
What’s the Difference Between Insurance Premiums and Other Out-of-Pocket Payments?
An insurance premium is the regular amount you pay to keep your policy active.
But there are other costs you might pay besides the premium. These are called out-of-pocket payments, and they usually apply to health or vehicle insurance. These include:
Deductible: This is the amount you pay before your insurance starts helping.
Copayment: A fixed amount you pay each time you use a service, like seeing a doctor.
Coinsurance: This is a part of the bill that you pay after the deductible is met.
In short, the premium keeps your policy running, and the other payments happen when you make a claim or use a service.
FAQs
Does an insurance premium count as an expense?
Yes, it is a regular cost you pay to keep your insurance policy active. But it’s also a way to protect your family from future financial stress, so it can be seen as a wise and valuable expense.
Can the insurance premium change during the tenure?
In most cases, your premium stays the same during the policy term. However, if you add extra riders or benefits, or switch plans, the terms and conditions may change.
Is any grace period available for payment of the premium?
Yes. Most life insurance policies give you a grace period after the due date. During this time, you can pay the missed premium and keep the policy active.
What are the options for paying the insurance premium online?
You can pay online using:
Debit card
Credit card
UPI
Net banking
Mobile wallets
Auto-debit from bank
Choose the one that’s easy and convenient for you.
Can I cancel my policy, and if so, will I receive a refund of my insurance premium?
Yes, you can cancel during the free-look period (usually 15 to 30 days from purchase). If cancelled in this time, you may get a refund after small deductions, as per policy rules.
Can you change the premium over the life of the policy?
Not for most standard plans. But for policies with flexible coverage or investment options, those can change based on market performance or other changes you make.
Can I claim during the grace period?
Yes, if a valid claim arises during the grace period, it might be honored.  However, it will only be honored if your premium was paid in the grace period.  If your claim is made after the grace period and you did not pay your premium by then, there is a strong probability that it will be denied due to the policy lapsing.
What is a Waiver of Premium Rider?
This is an extra feature you can add to your policy. If you suffer a serious disability or critical illness (as defined in the policy), the insurance company may waive future premiums under this rider.
Disclaimer
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale. It ensure that your loved ones remains financially secure even in case something unexpected happens to you. A financial safety net can make sure that your family’s life goals are not affected negatively.
What is Life Insurance?
Every person who is new to the world of insurance has had this question - what is life
insurance? In simple terms, life insurance is a contract between an individual and a life
insurance company. Under this contract, the insurance company is legally bound to pay out an
amount to the person nominated by the policyholder in case of the policyholder’s death.
There are different types of life insurance available in the Indian insurance market. Some, like
the term insurance plan, offer a pure life cover. That means that these types of life insurance
plans pay the nominee of the policyholder only on the policyholder’s death during the policy
term.
Other types of life insurance, like savings plans and retirement plans come with added benefits
that can help you fulfil the life goals at different life stages. These policies may also pay out the
benefits if the insured person outlives the policy term. In return for these death or maturity
benefits, the policyholder will have to pay life insurance premiums to the insurance company.
What is the meaning of this premium? Let’s find out.
What is the Meaning of Life Insurance Premium?
Life insurance premium is the amount that a policyholder pays the insurer to enjoy life
insurance coverage and other benefits. Only if the policyholder pays all the premiums on time
will the insurance company honour the contract and pay the policy benefits when they are due.
Depending on the terms and conditions of the policy, premiums can be paid as a lump sum
amount or in equal instalments. Life insurance plans also give policyholders the option to pay
the premiums on a monthly, quarterly, semi-annual, or annual basis. Again, this varies from one
plan to another.

When you buy an insurance policy, you pay a fixed amount of money. This amount is called a premium. As long as you pay the premium on time, your cover stays in place, which helps keep the policy active. You can choose how to pay the premium. Some people pay annually, while others pay monthly , half yearly, or quarterly. It depends on what suits their budget. If the premium is not paid on time, the policy may lapse, resulting in no coverage.
How is Life Insurance Premium Calculated?
The life insurance premium for different types of life insurance plans depends on a number of
factors. Insurance companies calculate the premium after taking into account various aspects
like the gender of the applicant, their age, the policy term, the lifestyle of the applicant and
sometimes, even their occupation etc.
Let’s take a closer look at how these factors affect the calculation of life insurance premium –
1. Gender
Women have been reported to have a higher life expectancy than men. So, life insurance
premiums charged for women tends to be lower than the charges levied for male policyholders
of the same age.
2. Age
Age, by itself, also influences the calculation of life insurance premiums. All life insurance
application forms will ask you for this information. This is because your age is a direct indicator
of your health and life expectancy, in most cases. As expected, younger people tend to be
healthier and live longer. So, the premiums charged for them are significantly lower as
compared to what’s charged for older people. The younger you are, the lower your life
insurance premiums will be.
For example, say you are 25 and you just got your first job. You purchase a life insurance plan
with your first salary, and the cover it offers is for 40 years. This means you will continue to pay
premiums until you are 65 years of age if you do not opt for a single payment option or a
limited payment option. Since 40 years is several years away, the premium charged will be
significantly lower than what somebody older who wishes to purchase the same life insurance
plan.
3. Policy term
The policy term is the duration over which the life insurance coverage remains in effect.
Generally, the longer the policy term, the more instalments your cost is spread over. So, the
premium charged per instalment will be higher for longer policy term.
4. Lifestyle
Lifestyle choices like smoking and drinking will lead to higher premiums because these habits
can reduce the life expectancy, resulting in early death. Similarly, other lifestyle choices like
engaging in adventurous sports that pose a high risk to life also increases the premiums.
5. Occupation
Some occupations are more dangerous than others. For example, a flight attendant’s job is
riskier than the job of somebody who works at a desk in an office. Occupations involving
physical risk typically lead to higher premiums.
Medical History
If you or your close family members have had serious health problems like cancer or heart disease, your premium may be high.
Height and Weight
Your height and weight are used to find out your Body Mass Index (BMI). If your BMI is too high or too low, it may lead to a higher premium.
Factors to Consider While Buying an Insurance Policy
Picking an insurance plan may depend on several things. You can think about your age, family needs, and how much you can afford to pay easily. Here are a few simple points to look at before you choose a plan:
Current Life Stage
The current stage of your life may affect your insurance needs. A single person, a married couple, or a parent with kidseach stage might have different coverage. Pick a policy that suits your current stage in life and has the potential to take care of your plans.
Financial Requirements
Think about how much your family will need in case you’re not around. Include education costs, or day- to-day expenses etc. Choose a cover that suits your income bracket and offers good financial protection .
Duration of the Policy
This is the number of years your life insurance will protect you. Choose the term based on your goals. For example, if you have young children, you may want coverage until they grow up and become independent.
Riders
Riders, also known as add-ons, are extra covers you can add to your bas policy. These provide additional protection, such as coverage for critical illness, accidents, or disability. You may consider purchasing these at an additional nominal cost to increase your coverage.
Claim Settlement Ratio
This shows how many claims an insurer has paid out compared to claims received in a financial year. A high Claim Settlement Ratio means the insurer is more likely to payout claims efficiently. So, it is beneficial to choose a company with a promising record when it comes to the Claim Settlement Ratio.
Terms and Conditions
Always read the policy document carefully. This will help you understand what is covered and what is not. Check the document for what will happen if you miss payments, want to claim money etc. Understanding the terms helps you avoid surprises later.
How to Pay Life Insurance Premiums?
You can pay your life insurance premium in different ways depending from Insurer to Insurer.:
● Pay online using UPI, debit card, credit card, or net banking ● Set up automatic payments
● Pay at the nearest insurance branch in cash or cheque ● Some insurers allow payment through mobile apps ● If you get insurance through work, the amount may be deducted from your salary
Choose an option that fits with your routine and helps you stay on track with your payments.
How Often Do You Pay Insurance Premiums?
You can choose how often you want to pay your premium:
● Monthly ● Quarterly (every 3 months) ● Half-yearly (every 6 months) ● Yearly (once a year)
Paying yearly might help you purchase insurance at an affordable price. But if monthly works better for your budget, that’s also fine. Choose what fits you best.
Tips to Reduce Your Life Insurance Premium
Now that you know the factors that play a key role in calculating life insurance premiums, here
are some ways in which you can reduce these costs –
● Ensure that you remain healthy and fit. Exercise regularly.
● Quit harmful lifestyle practices like smoking and drinking.
● Buy life insurance when you are younger and buy it online if you can.
FAQs
Does an insurance premium count as an expense?
Yes, it is a regular cost you pay to keep your insurance policy active. But it’s also a way to protect your family from future financial stress, so it can be seen as a wise and valuable expense.
Is any grace period available for payment of the premium?
Yes. Usually a grace period of 30 days ( where premium is paid in quarterly/half-yearly/annual instalments)and 15 days ( for monthly premiums) is provided after the premium due date. During this time, you can pay the missed premium and keep the policy active.
What are the options for paying the insurance premium online?
You can pay online with the below options depending from Insurer to Insurer :
● Debit card ● Credit card ● UPI ● Net banking ● Mobile wallets ● Auto-debit from bank
Choose the one that’s easy and convenient for you.
Can I cancel my policy, and if so, will I receive a refund of my insurance premium?
Yes, you can cancel during the free-look period (usually 30 days from date of purchase). If cancelled during this time, you will get a refund after small deductions, as per policy rules.
Can I make a claim during the grace period?
Yes, if a valid claim arises during the grace period, it might be honored. During the grace period, the policy is considered to be in-force and the risk cover continues without any interruption as per the terms and conditions of the policy.However, it will only be honored if your premium was paid in the grace period. If your claim is made after the grace period and you did not pay your premium by then it will be denied due to the policy lapsing.
What is a Waiver of Premium Rider?
This is an add on benefit you can add to your policy. In case of death, disability or critical illness (as defined in the rider ), the insurance company may waive future premiums under this rider , subject to rider terms and conditions.
Disclaimer
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on
www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale. Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Ltd., Regd. office Address: Bajaj Allianz House, Airport Road, Yerawada, Pune - 411006, Reg. No.: 116, CIN: U66010PN2001PLC015959, Call us on toll free No.: 1800 209 7272, Mail us: customercare@bajajallianz.co.in BJAZ-WEB-EC-15869/25

changes.word_original: These habits are detrimental to health, so insurance companies may charge high premiums.

changes.word_original: Nature of Employment
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Some jobs are more risky than others.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: For example, construction workers or those working with machines may have to pay more than office workers.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Risky jobs mean higher premiums.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Likelihood of Claims Being Made by the Person Insured
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you have made insurance claims in the past or your profile shows high chances of future claims, the premium may be higher.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Insurers review records before determining the premium.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If your BMI is too high or too low, it may pose health risks, which might lead to a higher premium.
changes.pdf_final: If your BMI is too high or too low, it may lead to a higher premium.

changes.word_original: Marital Status and Dependents
changes.pdf_final: Factors to Consider While Buying an Insurance Policy

changes.word_original: If you're married and have children, you may want to consider higher coverage due to dependents.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Hobbies with High Risks
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you enjoy adventure sports like skydiving, deep-sea diving, or racing, your premium may be calculated as higher because they carry a higher-than-average risk of injury.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Debts
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you have many loans, you might go for higher coverage to protect your family.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Higher coverage means a higher premium.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Insurers may also suggest a shorter payment term to reduce risk.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Types of Insurance Premiums
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: There are different ways to pay your insurance premium, based on what suits your needs and budget.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Let’s look at the common types of insurance premiums you may come across while purchasing one for yourself.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Life Insurance Premiums
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: This is the money you pay to keep your life cover active.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: It depends on your age, health, job, occupation, lifestyle and sum assured.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you smoke or drink, your premium may be higher.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: You can pay this premium monthly, yearly, or all at once, depending on your plan.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Health Insurance Premiums
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: This is the amount you pay to get health cover for you or your family.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: It protects you from hospital bills.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: When purchasing a premium health insurance plan, factors such as age, medical history, and plan type may affect your premium.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Some employers pay this premium as a benefit for their workers.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Auto Insurance Premiums
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Auto insurance premiums are what you pay to protect your car.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If your car gets damaged or stolen, your insurer pays for repairs or loss.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: The premium might depend on factors like:
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: The car type (expensive cars have higher premiums)
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Where you live (areas with more accidents or theft have higher rates)
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Your age (young or very old drivers may pay more)
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Homeowners Insurance Premiums
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If you own a home, this insurance protects it from damage due to fire, storms, or theft.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: The premium might depend on:
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: The value of your home
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Its size and age
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: The location (homes in risky areas like flood or storm zones have higher premiums)
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Factors to Consider While Buying an Insurance Premium Policy?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: A single person, a married couple, or a parent with kids, each stage might have different coverage.
changes.pdf_final: A single person, a married couple, or a parent with kidseach stage might have different coverage.

changes.word_original: Include home loans, education costs, or day-to-day expenses.
changes.pdf_final: Include education costs, or day- to-day expenses etc.

changes.word_original: Choose a cover that suits your income bracket and offers good protection without becoming a burden.
changes.pdf_final: Choose a cover that suits your income bracket and offers good financial protection .

changes.word_original: Riders, also known as add-ons, are extra covers you can add to your basic policy.
changes.pdf_final: Riders, also known as add-ons, are extra covers you can add to your bas policy.

changes.word_original: You may consider purchasing these at an extra cost to increase your coverage.
changes.pdf_final: You may consider purchasing these at an additional nominal cost to increase your coverage.

changes.word_original: This shows how many claims an insurer has paid out compared to claims received.
changes.pdf_final: This shows how many claims an insurer has paid out compared to claims received in a financial year.

changes.word_original: A high Claim Settlement Ratio means the company is good at paying what it promised.
changes.pdf_final: A high Claim Settlement Ratio means the insurer is more likely to payout claims efficiently.

changes.word_original: Check the document for what will happen if you miss payments, want to claim money, or cancel the plan.
changes.pdf_final: Check the document for what will happen if you miss payments, want to claim money etc.

changes.pdf_final: You can pay your life insurance premium in different ways depending from Insurer to Insurer.:

changes.word_original: How Does Deductible Affect Insurance Premium?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: A deductible is the amount you pay before the insurance company helps with a claim.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Here's how it affects your premium:
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If your deductible is high, your premium will be lower
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: If your deductible is low, your premium will be higher
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Why?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Because with a high deductible, you are taking on more risk, so the insurance company charges you less.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: But if you want the insurance company to start paying sooner, you’ll need to pay more for that benefit.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Before choosing a deductible, think about how much you can pay out of your own pocket during an emergency.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Now that you know the factors that play a key role in calculating life insurance premiums, here are some ways in which you can reduce these costs –
changes.pdf_final: Now that you know the factors that play a key role in calculating life insurance premiums, here

changes.word_original: Ensure that you remain healthy and fit.
changes.pdf_final: are some ways in which you can reduce these costs –
● Ensure that you remain healthy and fit.

changes.word_original: Quit harmful lifestyle practices like smoking and drinking.
changes.pdf_final: ● Quit harmful lifestyle practices like smoking and drinking.

changes.word_original: Buy life insurance when you are younger and buy it online if you can.
changes.pdf_final: ● Buy life insurance when you are younger and buy it online if you can.

changes.word_original: What’s the Difference Between Insurance Premiums and Other Out-of-Pocket Payments?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: An insurance premium is the regular amount you pay to keep your policy active.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: But there are other costs you might pay besides the premium.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: These are called out-of-pocket payments, and they usually apply to health or vehicle insurance.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: These include:
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Deductible: This is the amount you pay before your insurance starts helping.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Copayment: A fixed amount you pay each time you use a service, like seeing a doctor.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Coinsurance: This is a part of the bill that you pay after the deductible is met.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: In short, the premium keeps your policy running, and the other payments happen when you make a claim or use a service.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Can the insurance premium change during the tenure?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: In most cases, your premium stays the same during the policy term.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: However, if you add extra riders or benefits, or switch plans, the terms and conditions may change.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Most life insurance policies give you a grace period after the due date.
changes.pdf_final: Usually a grace period of 30 days ( where premium is paid in quarterly/half-yearly/annual instalments)and 15 days ( for monthly premiums) is provided after the premium due date.

changes.pdf_final: Yes, you can cancel during the free-look period (usually 30 days from date of purchase).

changes.word_original: If cancelled in this time, you may get a refund after small deductions, as per policy rules.
changes.pdf_final: If cancelled during this time, you will get a refund after small deductions, as per policy rules.

changes.word_original: Can you change the premium over the life of the policy?
changes.pdf_final: Can I make a claim during the grace period?

changes.word_original: Not for most standard plans.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: But for policies with flexible coverage or investment options, those can change based on market performance or other changes you make.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Can I claim during the grace period?
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: However, it will only be honored if your premium was paid in the grace period.
changes.pdf_final: During the grace period, the policy is considered to be in-force and the risk cover continues without any interruption as per the terms and conditions of the policy.However, it will only be honored if your premium was paid in the grace period.

changes.word_original: If your claim is made after the grace period and you did not pay your premium by then, there is a strong probability that it will be denied due to the policy lapsing.
changes.pdf_final: If your claim is made after the grace period and you did not pay your premium by then it will be denied due to the policy lapsing.

changes.pdf_final: In case of death, disability or critical illness (as defined in the rider ), the insurance company may waive future premiums under this rider , subject to rider terms and conditions.
